executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment has begun stabilizing after the post-PHE unwinding period, with 75.3 million enrollees reported as of January 2026. However, most major states continue experiencing enrollment declines, with year-over-year decreases ranging from 1.8% in New York to 7.6% in Michigan. California and Illinois show the steepest declines at 6.2%, while North Carolina stands as a notable outlier with modest growth of 0.5%. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, experienced a moderate 3.1% decline, positioning it as the third-largest state program. National per-enrollee spending averaged $2,869 in fiscal year 2024, with total expenditures reaching $215.9 billion and federal funding comprising $142.0 billion of this total.

Significant spending variations across states warrant attention, particularly the wide range in per-enrollee costs from Florida's $2,031 to Pennsylvania's $4,377. Texas demonstrates relatively efficient spending at $2,988 per enrollee—4% above the national average but substantially lower than comparable large states like New York ($3,431) and Ohio ($3,561). Quality performance remains concerning nationally, with an average score of 65.6 and 15 states scoring below 60. Texas ranks 30th with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below peer states like North Carolina and Florida. The state's managed care penetration of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for care coordination that should theoretically support better quality outcomes. Recent federal regulatory activity around HCBS quality measures and data collection requirements, combined with active OIG oversight, indicates increased federal scrutiny that will likely impact state program operations and reporting requirements in the coming fiscal year.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing 5.4% of the national total despite serving the second-most populous state. The program's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 sits modestly above the national average of $2,869 but significantly below expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). This cost efficiency reflects both Texas's non-expansion status and demographics, though it trails other non-expansion southern peers like North Carolina ($2,616). Texas's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, tracking closely with the national unwinding trend and performing better than large states like California (-6.2%) and Illinois (-6.2%), suggesting more controlled disenrollment processes as pandemic-era continuous coverage protections ended.

The program shows mixed performance indicators that merit close attention. Texas achieved an impressive 93.3% managed care penetration rate, well above the 83.2% national average, demonstrating strong care coordination infrastructure with nearly 3.9 million enrollees in managed plans. However, quality outcomes remain concerning, with Texas scoring 62/100 on CMS quality measures—ranking 30th nationally and falling into the "moderate" tier. While this exceeds bottom-performing southern states like Arkansas (54) and South Carolina (53), it significantly lags behind high-performing states and suggests substantial room for improvement in care delivery. The combination of high managed care adoption but mediocre quality scores indicates Texas should focus on enhancing accountability measures and performance standards within its managed care contracts, particularly as the program represents over $12 billion in annual expenditures serving a vulnerable population in a state that has not expanded Medicaid eligibility.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 significantly exceeds the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost management issues.

alerts.title: Florida Unusually Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Questions
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating underfunding or access issues.

alerts.title: Bottom 5 States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) scored below 55 on quality measures, with Mississippi at only 48 points.

alerts.title: High-Spending States Show Mixed Quality Performance
alerts.description: Pennsylvania and Ohio have high per-enrollee costs ($4,377 and $3,561 respectively) but lack corresponding quality scores in top tier.

executiveInsights.title: Texas HHSC quality score ranks #30 nationally at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality performance is below the national average of 65.6, ranking 30th out of 50 states. This puts Texas in the 'moderate' tier, significantly behind top performers like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and federal oversight intensity. Below-average scores increase regulatory scrutiny and may trigger mandatory improvement plans.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement opportunities to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee spending of $2,988 exceeds national average by $119
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869. With over 4M enrollees, this represents approximately $480M in excess spending annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs signal potential inefficiencies or service delivery issues that could face federal budget pressure. This creates opportunities for cost optimization consulting services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers and benchmark against efficient peer states like NC ($2,616)

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration of 93.3% leads national average by 10+ points
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration versus the 83.2% national average, with 3.86M enrollees in managed care plans. This represents best-in-class managed care adoption.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration demonstrates operational sophistication but may indicate readiness for advanced value-based care models and population health initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag advanced managed care optimization opportunities to account team

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% YoY, matching national unwinding trend
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped by 3.1% year-over-year to 4.03M enrollees, consistent with post-pandemic eligibility unwinding. This aligns with declines seen in most major states.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines reduce total contract value and may trigger operational efficiency requirements. HHSC will need support managing reduced scale while maintaining service levels.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review contract implications of continued enrollment decline with legal team

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 announced in Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services quality measures for 2028 implementation. This represents upcoming regulatory requirements for state Medicaid programs.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates, reporting changes, and compliance planning. Early preparation gives Accenture competitive advantage in helping HHSC adapt.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review Federal Register notice and assess HHSC readiness for 2028 HCBS requirements

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY could accelerate, reducing contract value and requiring operational adjustments.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop variable cost models and diversification strategies to offset potential revenue decline.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality improvement mandate
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally at 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for major performance improvements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture's quality improvement capabilities to help Texas reach top-tier performance levels.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration suggests upcoming contract renewals worth billions in Texas market.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and prepare comprehensive managed care modernization proposals.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality requirements will require significant system and process changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement solutions before the 2028 implementation deadline.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight could impact Texas operations or create compliance requirements for current contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Conduct proactive compliance review and strengthen audit response capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost is above national average, creating opportunity for efficiency improvements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost reduction initiatives targeting the gap between Texas and lower-cost states like Florida.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection activities for Medicaid and CHIP will require enhanced reporting capabilities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop automated data collection and reporting solutions for the expanded CMS requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive market pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple state procurement activities visible across platforms suggest increased competition for Medicaid technology contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen competitive positioning through unique IP development and strategic partnership formation.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% over the past 12 months, consistent with national unwinding trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spent $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee in fiscal year 2024, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care, above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, below the national average of 65.6.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two Texas-specific procurement portals are actively monitoring opportunities: HHSC portal and Electronic State Business Daily.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Three active OIG/regulatory items are being monitored including HHS OIG oversight and recent Medicaid program announcements.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestone dates are provided in the current data.
